children.name: Macaca
children.description: The most geographically widespread primate genus after Homo, with over 20 species distributed from Gibraltar to Japan. Macaques are highly adaptable, thriving in tropical forests, snowy mountains, and urban environments alike, and are widely used in biomedical research.

children.name: Macaca mulatta
children.description: The most widely studied non-human primate, native to South and East Asia from Afghanistan to China. Rhesus macaques live in large multi-male troops and are famous for their role in biomedical research, including the discovery of the Rh blood factor.

children.name: Macaca fascicularis
children.description: Also called the long-tailed macaque, this highly adaptable species is distributed across Southeast Asia from Myanmar to the Philippines. It is one of the most abundant primates in the region and is notorious for raiding crops and entering human settlements.

children.name: Macaca fuscata
children.description: The northernmost non-human primate, found on three of Japan's main islands. The Jigokudani population is renowned for bathing in hot springs during winter, a behaviour that has spread culturally through the troop.

children.name: Macaca sylvanus
children.description: The only macaque found outside Asia, native to the cedar and oak forests of Morocco, Algeria, and the introduced population on the Rock of Gibraltar. Males play an unusual role in infant care, carrying and presenting infants to other males as a bonding strategy.

children.name: Macaca nigra
children.description: An entirely black macaque endemic to the northeastern tip of Sulawesi, Indonesia, and the Togian Islands. Critically endangered due to hunting for bushmeat and habitat destruction; its distinctive crest and red facial skin make it unmistakable.

children.name: Macaca nemestrina
children.description: A large macaque of Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, and Borneo, recognisable by its short, pig-like tail. Traditionally trained in some regions to harvest coconuts; it plays an important role as a seed disperser in lowland rainforests.

children.name: Macaca thibetana
children.description: The largest macaque species, found in the montane forests and rocky terrain of central and eastern China. Males can weigh up to 30 kg; the species forms large mixed-sex troops and is well known from tourist sites at Mt. Emei and Mt. Huangshan.

children.name: Macaca arctoides
children.description: A stocky, bare-faced macaque with a very short tail found from northeastern India through southern China to Peninsular Malaysia. Adults develop a bald, deep-red face; the species shows a notably tolerant social style with frequent reconciliation after conflicts.

children.name: Macaca assamensis
children.description: A medium-sized macaque of montane forests from Nepal and northeastern India through Southeast Asia to southwestern China. It occupies altitudes up to 3500 m and is distinguished by a relatively short tail and pale facial skin.

children.name: Macaca radiata
children.description: Endemic to peninsular India south of the Godavari River, named for the hair that radiates outward from a central whorl on the crown. Highly commensal, bonnet macaques frequently live in and around human settlements and temples.

children.name: Macaca sinica
children.description: Endemic to Sri Lanka, with a distinctive whorl of hair on the crown resembling a toque cap. The Polonnaruwa population has been the subject of decades of continuous research on primate social behaviour and ecology.

children.name: Macaca silenus
children.description: The lion-tailed macaque, locally called wanderoo, is an Old World monkey endemic to the Western Ghats of South India.

children.name: Macaca tonkeana
children.description: Endemic to central Sulawesi, the Tonkean macaque is one of seven macaque species found on the island. It has a highly tolerant social style characterised by frequent affiliative contact and rare escalated aggression.

children.name: Macaca maura
children.description: Restricted to the southwestern peninsula of Sulawesi, the moor macaque is endangered due to forest clearance for agriculture. Its pink, hairless ischial swellings are visible in both sexes and function in social signalling.

children.name: Macaca ochreata
children.description: Found in southeastern Sulawesi and the Buton Island group, the booted macaque is named for the contrasting pale lower legs that resemble boots. It inhabits lowland and hill forest and is poorly studied compared to other Sulawesi macaques.

children.name: Macaca nigrescens
children.description: Endemic to the Gorontalo and northern arm region of Sulawesi, occurring parapatrically with Macaca nigra to the east and east of it with M. tonkeana. One of the least-studied Sulawesi macaques, its conservation status is Vulnerable.

children.name: Macaca hecki
children.description: A macaque from the northern arm of Sulawesi, in the contact zone between M. nigrescens and M. tonkeana; hybridisation at range boundaries has made species limits contentious. Named after Heini Heck, director of the Munich Zoo.

children.name: Macaca leonina
children.description: Formerly a subspecies of M. nemestrina, the northern pig-tailed macaque ranges from northeastern India and southern China through Indochina and Thailand. It is important as a seed disperser in Southeast Asian forests and is listed as Vulnerable.

children.name: Macaca pagensis
children.description: Endemic to the southern Mentawai Islands off Sumatra, the Pagai macaque is Critically Endangered with a population of perhaps 3,000 individuals. It is heavily hunted for food by local communities and has lost much of its forest habitat to logging.

children.name: Macaca siberu
children.description: The Siberut macaque is a vulnerable species of macaque, which is endemic to Siberut Island in Indonesia. It was formerly considered conspecific with the Pagai Island macaque which is overall paler in color, but this arrangement was polyphyletic. Both were formerly considered subspecies of the southern pig-tailed macaque.

children.name: Macaca cyclopis
children.description: Endemic to Taiwan, where it inhabits montane forests from sea level to 3,600 m. The only native primate of Taiwan; populations in urban fringe areas have become habituated to humans and sometimes raid crops and orchards.

children.name: Macaca munzala
children.description: The Arunachal macaque is a macaque native to Eastern Himalayas of Bhutan, China and India. It is listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List.

children.name: Macaca brunnescens
children.description: Found on Buton Island and several small adjacent islands off southeastern Sulawesi, sometimes treated as a subspecies of M. ochreata. Its restricted range and ongoing habitat loss place it in the Vulnerable category on the IUCN Red List.

children.name: Papio
children.description: Baboons are large, terrestrial Old World monkeys native to the open woodlands and savannas of Africa and Arabia. They live in multi-male, multi-female troops of up to 200 individuals and are among the most successful primates on the continent.

children.name: Papio anubis
children.description: The most widely distributed baboon species, ranging from Mali and Ethiopia south to Tanzania across sub-Saharan savanna and woodland. Olive baboons live in large, complex troops and are notable for the formation of coalitions among males and cross-species friendships with humans.

children.name: Papio cynocephalus
children.description: A slender, long-limbed baboon of eastern and central Africa, found in dry savannas, bushland, and open woodland. Yellow baboons hybridise extensively with olive baboons where their ranges overlap, making Amboseli one of the best-studied hybrid zones in primatology.

children.name: Papio ursinus
children.description: The largest baboon, found across southern Africa from Zimbabwe to the Cape. Chacma baboons display one of the most despotic dominance hierarchies among baboons and are infamous for infanticide by incoming males; the Cape Town population raids homes and is considered a pest.

children.name: Papio hamadryas
children.description: The sacred baboon of ancient Egypt, depicted on tomb walls and mummified in temple offerings. Males have a striking silver cape and lead harem units of one to several females; hamadryas baboons extend into the Arabian Peninsula, the only baboon found in Asia.

children.name: Papio kindae
children.description: The smallest baboon species, recognised as a full species only in 2013, ranging across Zambia, southern DRC, Angola, and Tanzania. Kinda baboons show unusually tolerant male–female relationships, with males providing care and protection to preferred female partners.

children.name: Papio papio
children.description: The smallest-ranged baboon, restricted to the westernmost part of the African savanna from Senegal and Gambia to Guinea-Bissau and southern Mauritania. Guinea baboons have a unique social system organised around nested parties, bands, and gangs rather than the harem or troop structures of other species.

children.name: Mandrillus
children.description: The mandrill is the world's largest monkey and the most colourful mammal, with males displaying vivid blue and red facial markings that intensify with dominance. Both species inhabit the rainforests of west-central Africa and live in large multi-male groups.

children.name: Mandrillus sphinx
children.description: The largest monkey species in the world, males weighing up to 54 kg and bearing brilliant blue and red facial skin that intensifies in dominant individuals. Mandrills live in the rainforests of equatorial Africa from Cameroon to the Congo and form enormous hordes of several hundred individuals.

children.name: Mandrillus leucophaeus
children.description: The drill is a close relative of the mandrill, restricted to lowland forests in Cameroon, Nigeria, and Bioko Island. Critically Endangered due to intensive hunting for bushmeat and habitat loss; considered one of Africa's most endangered primates, with fewer than 3,000 individuals remaining.

children.name: Theropithecus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the gelada, a grass-eating monkey of the Ethiopian highlands and the only surviving member of a once-diverse lineage. Geladas live in the largest primate groups of any non-human, with herds of up to 600 individuals.

children.name: Theropithecus gelada
children.description: A highly specialised grass-grazer confined to the Ethiopian highlands above 1,800 m, the gelada uses a unique shuffle-feeding technique to harvest blades and seeds. Males bear a bright red hourglass patch of bare skin on the chest that functions as a visual signal, replacing the genital signals of most other cercopithecines.

children.name: Cercocebus
children.description: Mangabeys are medium-sized, semi-terrestrial monkeys of African lowland forests, closely related to mandrills and baboons. They are specialist hard-object feeders, with robust jaws and deep molar reliefs allowing them to crack seeds and pods that other monkeys cannot exploit.

children.name: Cercocebus torquatus
children.description: A coastal forest mangabey from Nigeria, Cameroon, Gabon, and the Congo, recognisable by a chestnut-red crown contrasting with its grey body. It forages both in the canopy and on the ground and is Vulnerable due to hunting and deforestation.

children.name: Cercocebus atys
children.description: A grey-brown mangabey of the Upper Guinea forests from Gambia to Ghana, well known as a natural host of the simian immunodeficiency virus that gave rise to HIV-2 in humans. Large troops of up to 100 individuals forage on the forest floor and in low vegetation.

children.name: Cercocebus agilis
children.description: Found in swampy and gallery forests from Cameroon and the Central African Republic to northern DRC, the agile mangabey is primarily terrestrial and semi-aquatic. It is named for its nimble movements on the forest floor and can swim to cross rivers.

children.name: Cercocebus chrysogaster
children.description: Endemic to the Congo Basin rainforests south of the Congo River, formerly treated as a subspecies of C. agilis. Distinguished by a golden-yellow ventral surface; it inhabits swamp forest and is one of the least-studied mangabey species.

children.name: Cercocebus galeritus
children.description: Critically Endangered and restricted to the gallery forests along a 60 km stretch of the lower Tana River in Kenya, with only a few hundred individuals remaining. Its habitat is threatened by agricultural conversion and dam projects that alter the flood regime.

children.name: Cercocebus lunulatus
children.description: Restricted to forests in Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire, formerly treated as a subspecies of C. atys. Critically Endangered with population decline driven by bushmeat hunting and conversion of forests to cocoa and oil palm plantations.

children.name: Lophocebus
children.description: Black mangabeys are arboreal Old World monkeys of central African forests, more closely related to baboons than to the semi-terrestrial Cercocebus mangabeys despite their similar appearance — a classic example of convergent evolution. They bear conspicuous crests and eyelid colouration.

children.name: Lophocebus albigena
children.description: A large, predominantly arboreal mangabey of lowland rainforests from Cameroon to Uganda and Kenya. Grey-cheeked mangabeys are prominent members of polyspecific associations and are known for their far-carrying 'whoop-gobble' contact calls.

children.name: Lophocebus aterrimus
children.description: Found in the Congo Basin rainforests of DRC, distinguished by a prominent black crest and dark pelage. Forages at multiple canopy levels; relatively little studied in the wild and assessed as Least Concern, though threatened by bushmeat hunting.

children.name: Lophocebus johnstoni
children.description: Sometimes treated as a subspecies of L. albigena, Johnston's mangabey is found in the montane and lowland forests of eastern DRC, Rwanda, and Burundi. Named after the explorer and administrator Sir Harry Johnston.

children.name: Lophocebus opdenboschi
children.description: A poorly known mangabey described from the forest south of the Congo River in southwestern DRC. One of the least-studied cercopithecid species, known from limited specimens; its taxonomic status relative to other Lophocebus has been debated.

children.name: Rungwecebus
children.description: A monotypic genus erected in 2005 for the kipunji, the first new African monkey genus described in 83 years. Discovered independently by two field teams in Tanzania's Southern Highlands and Udzungwa Mountains, the kipunji was initially misidentified as a Lophocebus mangabey.

children.name: Rungwecebus kipunji
children.description: Critically Endangered and confined to two small, isolated populations in the Rungwe-Livingstone forests and Ndundulu forest of Tanzania, totalling fewer than 1,500 individuals. Kipunjis live in montane forest above 1,500 m and produce a distinctive 'honk-bark' call audible over 1 km.

children.name: Cercopithecus
children.description: The guenons — a large, diverse genus of small to medium-sized forest monkeys — are the most species-rich primate genus in Africa. Each species typically has a distinctive facial pattern, which facilitates species recognition in the mixed-species groups they often form.

children.name: Cercopithecus mitis
children.description: One of the most widespread guenons, found in forests from Ethiopia south to South Africa. Blue monkeys are not blue — the common name refers to the blue-grey appearance of the face at close range; they are important in mixed-species primate associations.

children.name: Cercopithecus diana
children.description: A strikingly patterned guenon of the Upper Guinea rainforests from Sierra Leone to Ghana, with a white throat, chestnut back, and black-and-white facial markings. Diana monkeys are sentinel species in polyspecific groups and respond to the alarm calls of other species.

children.name: Cercopithecus neglectus
children.description: A distinctive guenon with a white beard, orange diadem, and grey body, found in swamp and riverine forests from Cameroon to Ethiopia and Angola. Males maintain territories via loud calls and gland-marking, and are noted for their unusually secretive and elusive behaviour.

children.name: Cercopithecus nictitans
children.description: A large guenon of the lowland rainforests of west-central Africa, recognisable by a conspicuous white spot on the nose. One of the most vocal guenons, with a complex repertoire including distinct calls for different predator types.

children.name: Cercopithecus petaurista
children.description: Found in the Upper Guinea forests of West Africa, from Senegal to Togo. Like the greater spot-nosed monkey, it bears a white nasal spot; the two species regularly associate in polyspecific troops and use each other's alarm calls.

children.name: Cercopithecus ascanius
children.description: A small, active guenon of central and east African forests, immediately identifiable by its chestnut-red tail and white nose spot. Red-tailed monkeys are frequent members of polyspecific associations and are one of the most abundant forest monkeys of the Congo Basin.

children.name: Cercopithecus cephus
children.description: Endemic to the lowland forests of Cameroon, Gabon, and the Republic of Congo, with vivid blue facial skin and a white moustache stripe. Moustached monkeys are characteristic members of west-central African polyspecific associations.

children.name: Cercopithecus mona
children.description: Native to forests from Ghana to Cameroon but also introduced to the Caribbean island of Grenada in the 17th century, where it has established a feral population. An early introduced primate to the New World, mona monkeys are now a cultural symbol of Grenada.

children.name: Cercopithecus campbelli
children.description: A forest guenon of West Africa from Gambia to Ghana, studied for its sophisticated alarm call system in Côte d'Ivoire. Males produce distinct calls for eagles and leopards, and can combine root calls with suffixes to create new meanings — an early example of primate proto-syntax.

children.name: Cercopithecus lowei
children.description: Originally described as a subspecies of C. campbelli, Lowe's monkey is now treated as a full species restricted to forests east of the Sassandra River in Côte d'Ivoire and western Ghana. Named after William P. Lowe.

children.name: Cercopithecus pogonias
children.description: A medium-sized guenon found in the lowland forests of the Niger Delta, Cameroon, Gabon, and the Congo, bearing a distinctive black crown stripe. Crowned monkeys are regular participants in polyspecific groups alongside other forest guenons.

children.name: Cercopithecus erythrogaster
children.description: Restricted to the forests of southern Nigeria and Benin, this Vulnerable guenon is one of Africa's lesser-known primates. The rust-coloured belly contrasts sharply with the white throat and dark dorsal pelage, making it distinctive in the field.

children.name: Cercopithecus erythrotis
children.description: A guenon of the forests of Bioko Island and adjacent mainland Cameroon and Nigeria, recognisable by its vivid red ear patches and blue facial skin. Bioko populations are heavily hunted for bushmeat and are Vulnerable.

children.name: Cercopithecus hamlyni
children.description: Found in the montane and bamboo forests of eastern DRC and Rwanda, with a distinctive owl-like face patterned in silver-grey and olive. Largely terrestrial for a guenon; the species is poorly known due to its remote and often war-affected habitat.

children.name: Cercopithecus wolfi
children.description: A guenon of the Congo Basin south of the Congo River, with a vivid yellow-white diadem and red tail. Named after the naturalist Ludwig Wolf; it is closely related to C. denti and both are sometimes treated as subspecies of a broader C. mona group.

children.name: Cercopithecus denti
children.description: Distributed in the forests of the northeastern Congo Basin in DRC, Uganda, and western Kenya. Named after Major C. T. Dent; it closely resembles C. wolfi and is a member of the mona-group of guenons.

children.name: Cercopithecus dryas
children.description: One of Africa's rarest and least-known primates, described in 1932 and confirmed alive only in 2007 by camera trap in the central Congo Basin. It is Endangered and confined to a small area south of the Congo River; its taxonomic placement has long been uncertain.

children.name: Cercopithecus roloway
children.description: Critically Endangered and restricted to forest remnants in Ghana and Côte d'Ivoire; some surveys consider it functionally extinct in Ghana. Formerly treated as a subspecies of C. diana, it differs in its white beard shape and is a priority conservation target.

children.name: Cercopithecus preussi
children.description: A montane forest guenon found on Bioko Island, in the Cameroon Highlands, and Cross River forests of Nigeria. Endangered due to habitat loss and hunting; named after the German colonial administrator Paul Preuss.

children.name: Cercopithecus doggetti
children.description: Found in the montane forests of Rwanda, Burundi, and adjacent DRC, sometimes treated as a subspecies of C. mitis. Silver monkeys are named for the frosted silver-grey colouration of their dorsal pelage and are important frugivores in Afromontane forests.

children.name: Cercopithecus kandti
children.description: An Endangered guenon endemic to the bamboo and montane forests of the Virunga Volcanoes and Gishwati Forest of Rwanda, DRC, and Uganda. The golden-orange flanks and back make it one of Africa's most striking primates; it is a flagship species for Volcanoes National Park.

children.name: Cercopithecus sclateri
children.description: Restricted to lowland forests of southeastern Nigeria, this Endangered species was known only from a few museum specimens until live populations were rediscovered in the 1980s. Named after the zoologist Philip Sclater; it is one of Nigeria's rarest primates.

children.name: Allochrocebus
children.description: A small genus of ground-dwelling guenons of central and west-central Africa, separated from Cercopithecus based on molecular and morphological evidence. These species are more terrestrial than typical guenons and inhabit montane and submontane forests.

children.name: Allochrocebus lhoesti
children.description: A montane forest monkey found in the Albertine Rift from the Virungas to southwestern Uganda and eastern DRC, spending much of its time on or near the ground. Named after the Belgian zoologist M. L'Hoest; groups are led by females and males are more peripheral.

children.name: Allochrocebus solatus
children.description: Discovered only in 1984 in the Forêt des Abeilles of central Gabon and described formally in 1988. Named 'solatus' (isolated) for its disjunct range; it bears a distinctive orange tail tip and is primarily terrestrial in closed-canopy rainforest.

children.name: Allochrocebus preussi
children.description: A montane forest species of the Cameroon Highlands and Bioko Island, closely related to A. lhoesti and formerly treated as a subspecies. It occupies high-altitude forests above 1,200 m and is Endangered due to hunting and forest clearance.

children.name: Erythrocebus
children.description: The patas monkey is the fastest-running primate, capable of reaching 55 km/h. A specialised open-country species of the African savanna belt, it is the most terrestrial of the guenon-like monkeys and has long limbs adapted for ground locomotion.

children.name: Erythrocebus patas
children.description: The fastest primate on Earth, native to the dry savannas and semi-arid grasslands across sub-Saharan Africa from Senegal to Ethiopia. Groups typically contain one adult male who serves as a sentinel while females forage; the male's conspicuous presence is thought to be a decoy tactic for predators.

children.name: Miopithecus
children.description: The talapoins are the smallest Old World monkeys, with a round head, large eyes, and a bright green coat. They are highly social and semi-aquatic, living in large groups along rivers and flooded forests of equatorial Africa.

children.name: Miopithecus talapoin
children.description: The smaller of the two talapoin species, found in the coastal forests and mangroves of Angola and the mouth of the Congo River. Groups of 60–100 individuals sleep in riverine vegetation and can swim readily; among primates, talapoins have the largest brain relative to body mass.

children.name: Miopithecus ogouensis
children.description: The Gabon talapoin is found in the riverine and swamp forests of Cameroon, Gabon, and the Republic of Congo, named after the Ogooué River. Separated from M. talapoin in 1996; slightly larger and more northerly, it is the species most commonly kept in European laboratories.

children.name: Allenopithecus
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Allen's swamp monkey, the most plesiomorphic cercopithecine and a morphological outlier among the guenon lineage. It is placed in its own subfamily by some authorities due to its distinctive anatomy and behaviour.

children.name: Allenopithecus nigroviridis
children.description: A compact, semi-aquatic monkey of the Congo Basin swamp and inundated forests, named after the zoologist Joel Asaph Allen. It has interdigital webbing and can swim well; it feeds heavily on aquatic invertebrates, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Chlorocebus
children.description: Vervet monkeys are among the most studied primates in the wild, renowned for their distinct alarm calls that discriminate between aerial and terrestrial predators. Highly adaptable, they occupy habitats from forest edge to semi-arid savanna across sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Chlorocebus pygerythrus
children.description: The archetypal East and southern African monkey of open woodland and savanna, extensively studied for its alarm call semantics and social behaviour. Males display vivid blue scrotal colouration; vervets are among the most abundant primates in Africa and readily commensal with humans.

children.name: Chlorocebus sabaeus
children.description: A uniformly greenish-grey monkey of West Africa from Senegal to the Volta River, also naturalised in the Caribbean (Barbados, St Kitts, and Nevis) following the slave trade era. Green monkeys are widely used in SIV and flavivirus research.

children.name: Chlorocebus aethiops
children.description: The grivet is found in dry woodland and savanna from Sudan and Eritrea to Ethiopia, characterised by distinctive white cheek tufts. It is considered the most ancestral of the Chlorocebus species in terms of range position; it hybridises with C. tantalus where ranges meet.

children.name: Chlorocebus tantalus
children.description: Found across the savanna zone of sub-Saharan Africa from Ghana to Uganda, often considered the central species within Chlorocebus. Tantalus monkeys are named for the mythological figure and are common crop raiders across the Sahel and Guinea woodland zone.

children.name: Chlorocebus cynosuros
children.description: A vervet-like monkey of the miombo woodlands and river valleys of Angola, Zambia, Malawi, and the DRC. Named after the Duke of Marlborough (Malbrouck); it was long regarded as a subspecies of C. pygerythrus but is now recognised as a distinct species.

children.name: Chlorocebus djamdjamensis
children.description: Restricted to the Afromontane bamboo forests of the Bale Mountains in southern Ethiopia, at altitudes of 2,400–3,600 m. One of the least-known Chlorocebus species; specialised for bamboo habitat and at risk from livestock grazing and deforestation.

children.name: Colobus
children.description: Colobus monkeys are the only Old World monkeys that lack a thumb, an adaptation for rapid arboreal movement through forest canopies. They are specialist leaf-eaters with complex, multi-chambered stomachs that ferment tough foliage.

children.name: Colobus guereza
children.description: The most familiar black-and-white colobus, widespread in riverine and montane forests from Nigeria to Ethiopia. The flowing white mantle and tail tassel were prized for headdresses and cloaks; guerezas are prominent folivores in east African forests.

children.name: Colobus polykomos
children.description: Found in the Upper Guinea forests of West Africa from Gambia to Côte d'Ivoire. It has a white brow band but lacks the distinctive mantle of C. guereza; heavily exploited for its pelt and bushmeat and considered Vulnerable.

children.name: Colobus angolensis
children.description: A black-and-white colobus of the forests of east-central Africa, from Kenya and Tanzania south to Angola, with seven recognised subspecies. The white shoulder patches vary considerably across subspecies and populations.

children.name: Colobus satanas
children.description: The only entirely black colobus, found in the lowland rainforests of Cameroon, Gabon, the Republic of Congo, and Bioko Island. It subsists heavily on seeds rather than leaves, and its large home ranges are a consequence of low food density; classified as Vulnerable.

children.name: Colobus vellerosus
children.description: A white-thighed colobus found in Ghana, Togo, Benin, and southwestern Nigeria, sometimes treated as a subspecies of C. polykomos. Subject of a notable intergroup infanticide study at Boabeng-Fiema Monkey Sanctuary; assessed as Near Threatened.

children.name: Piliocolobus
children.description: Red colobus monkeys, among the most hunted primates in Africa and a preferred prey of chimpanzees. Several species are critically endangered due to habitat loss and hunting pressure.

children.name: Piliocolobus badius
children.description: Found in the Upper Guinea rainforests from Gambia to Ghana, one of the most hunted primates in West Africa. Chimpanzees in Tai Forest conduct coordinated hunts targeting this species; the population has declined dramatically due to habitat loss and hunting.

children.name: Piliocolobus tephrosceles
children.description: Common in Kibale Forest, Uganda, where it is the preferred prey of chimpanzees and the subject of extensive research on predator-prey dynamics. Groups of up to 80 individuals form and are frequently joined by other colobine and cercopithecine species.

children.name: Piliocolobus rufomitratus
children.description: Critically Endangered and restricted to riverine forests along 60 km of the lower Tana River in Kenya, with fewer than 1,000 individuals. Shares its highly fragmented habitat with the similarly endangered Tana River mangabey.

children.name: Piliocolobus kirkii
children.description: Endemic to Zanzibar Island, Tanzania, and Endangered with a population of around 4,000 individuals. Unguja Island forests are its stronghold; the species is notable for eating charcoal and exotic plants, thought to counteract plant toxins in its diet.

children.name: Piliocolobus gordonorum
children.description: Endemic to the Udzungwa Mountains of Tanzania, with fragmented populations in a few protected forest blocks. Vulnerable to hunting and forest fragmentation; named in honour of the Gordon family who supported conservation in the Udzungwas.

children.name: Piliocolobus foai
children.description: A species complex of red colobus distributed across the Congo Basin from DRC to Zambia and Tanzania. Named after Édouard Foa, a French explorer; it is the most widespread red colobus and one of the most taxonomically variable.

children.name: Piliocolobus pennantii
children.description: Restricted to Bioko Island, Equatorial Guinea, where it is Critically Endangered due to heavy bushmeat hunting, especially during the bioko hunting season. Named after the naturalist Thomas Pennant; one of the world's 25 most endangered primates.

children.name: Piliocolobus oustaleti
children.description: Found in forests of the Central African Republic, southern Sudan, and northern DRC, named after the French zoologist Émile Oustalet. It is one of the less studied red colobus taxa and its taxonomy remains unsettled.

children.name: Piliocolobus preussi
children.description: Critically Endangered, restricted to forests in the Cross River area of Cameroon and Nigeria. Named after the German collector Paul Preuss; one of the world's rarest primates with a population of perhaps 400 individuals.

children.name: Piliocolobus langi
children.description: A red colobus of the eastern Congo Basin forests, named after Herbert Lang of the American Museum Congo Expedition. Its taxonomy relative to P. foai is contested; it inhabits lowland and montane forest in northeastern DRC.

children.name: Procolobus
children.description: A monotypic genus for the olive colobus, the smallest colobine monkey and one of the most cryptic primates of the West African rainforest. The olive colobus is the only colobine to carry its young in its mouth rather than clinging to the ventrum.

children.name: Procolobus verus
children.description: The smallest colobine, found in forest understoreys from Sierra Leone to Nigeria, with an inconspicuous olive-brown coat that makes it difficult to detect. Olive colobus often associate with Diana monkeys, apparently using them as a sentinel against predators.

children.name: Semnopithecus
children.description: The grey langurs or Hanuman langurs are among the most widespread leaf monkeys of South Asia, venerated in Hinduism as representatives of the god Hanuman. They are highly tolerant of human presence and can be found in cities, temples, and farmland as well as forests.

children.name: Semnopithecus entellus
children.description: The best-known and most widespread Hanuman langur, found across the Gangetic plain of northern India and adjacent Nepal. Sacred to Hindus, these langurs live freely in temples and cities; they were famously studied for infanticide by incoming males at Jodhpur.

children.name: Semnopithecus schistaceus
children.description: Found in the Himalayan forests of Nepal, northern India, Pakistan, and Afghanistan, at altitudes up to 4,000 m. One of the high-altitude specialists among langurs; its thick grey pelage helps it survive cold montane winters.

children.name: Semnopithecus priam
children.description: Found in southeastern India and Sri Lanka, with a distinctive tuft of hair on the crown and a whitish face. The Sri Lankan subspecies (S. p. thersites) is confined to the dry zone and is Vulnerable due to habitat fragmentation.

children.name: Semnopithecus dussumieri
children.description: Found in peninsular India south of the Narmada River, one of the most common langurs of the Deccan Plateau. Named after the French naturalist Jean-Jacques Dussumier; it thrives in human-modified habitats and is one of the most commensal non-human primates in India.

children.name: Semnopithecus hector
children.description: A grey langur of the terai foothills from Nepal to Assam, found at the base of the Himalayas in lowland sal forests. It forms large multi-male troops and has been studied at sites such as Chitwan National Park in Nepal.

children.name: Semnopithecus hypoleucos
children.description: Found in the forests of the Western Ghats and southern coastal India, distinguished by contrasting dark extremities against the pale grey body. Near Threatened due to habitat fragmentation in one of the world's biodiversity hotspots.

children.name: Semnopithecus ajax
children.description: A large, pale grey langur of the temperate forests of Kashmir, Himachal Pradesh, and adjacent Pakistan, inhabiting mixed cedar and oak forests at 1,500–3,600 m. Among the largest langur species and well adapted to cold climates.

children.name: Trachypithecus
children.description: Lutungs, or banded leaf monkeys — a diverse group of colobine monkeys distributed across South and Southeast Asia. Many species are born bright orange and gradually darken to their adult colouration, creating striking nursery groups within troops.

children.name: Trachypithecus cristatus
children.description: One of the most widespread lutungs, distributed across Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, Borneo, and adjacent islands. Silvery-grey adults contrast with the vivid orange neonates; the species is common in mangroves, coastal forests, and secondary vegetation.

children.name: Trachypithecus phayrei
children.description: Distributed from northeastern India and Myanmar through Indochina to Yunnan, China. Named after Sir Arthur Phayre, British commissioner of Burma; it is Endangered in parts of its range due to forest loss and hunting.

children.name: Trachypithecus obscurus
children.description: A medium-sized langur of Peninsular Malaysia and southern Thailand, common in primary and secondary forest, parks, and temple groves. The white eye-rings and mouth patches give the face a spectacled appearance; it is one of the most frequently studied primates of Southeast Asia.

children.name: Trachypithecus auratus
children.description: Endemic to Java, Bali, and Lombok in Indonesia, with two subspecies differing in pelage colour. Neonates are bright orange; adults are mostly black with varying amounts of golden pelage on the outer limbs. Vulnerable due to forest loss on densely populated Java.

children.name: Trachypithecus francoisi
children.description: A striking black langur with white sideburns found in the karst limestone forests of southern China, northeastern Vietnam, and adjacent Laos. Endangered due to habitat destruction and hunting for traditional medicine; named after the French consul Auguste François.

children.name: Trachypithecus delacouri
children.description: Critically Endangered and restricted to limestone karst forests in Ninh Bình Province, Vietnam, with fewer than 250 individuals. One of the world's rarest primates, named after the ornithologist Jean Théodore Delacour; its distinctive black-and-white patterning makes it unmistakable.

children.name: Trachypithecus poliocephalus
children.description: Critically Endangered, restricted to limestone islands in Cat Ba National Park, Vietnam, and on the Chinese island of Hainan. The Cat Ba subspecies has a population of around 60–70 individuals, making it one of the world's rarest primates.

children.name: Trachypithecus germaini
children.description: Found in Cambodia, southern Vietnam, and Thailand, sometimes treated as a subspecies of T. obscurus. Named after the explorer Isidore Germain; it inhabits dry deciduous and mixed deciduous forests and is Endangered due to hunting and habitat loss.

children.name: Trachypithecus pileatus
children.description: A widespread langur from Bangladesh, northeastern India, and Myanmar, named for the dark cap on its head. It inhabits tropical and subtropical moist forests up to 2,000 m and is Vulnerable in parts of its range due to habitat loss.

children.name: Trachypithecus johnii
children.description: A black-furred, golden-headed langur endemic to the forests of the Western Ghats and Nilgiri Hills of southern India. Vulnerable due to habitat fragmentation and hunting for its pelt and flesh used in traditional medicine.

children.name: Trachypithecus vetulus
children.description: Endemic to Sri Lanka, recognisable by its dark purple-brown bare face and white beard. Endangered and highly fragmented across the island's wet zone; the southwestern subspecies faces severe pressure from urbanisation in and around Colombo.

children.name: Trachypithecus hatinhensis
children.description: Found in the karst limestone forests along the Vietnam–Laos border in Ha Tinh and Quang Binh Provinces. Critically Endangered and heavily hunted; the species is morphologically and genetically distinct from adjacent T. laotum.

children.name: Trachypithecus laotum
children.description: A karst limestone langur found in central Laos, described from specimens collected by French naturalists. Endangered due to hunting for traditional medicine and loss of its limestone forest habitat.

children.name: Trachypithecus geei
children.description: Restricted to a small area of Assam, India, and adjacent Bhutan, with a beautiful creamy-golden coat that darkens to orange in the dry season. Endangered and sacred to local communities; named after the naturalist Edward Pritchard Gee who first brought it to scientific attention.

children.name: Trachypithecus shortridgei
children.description: Found in the Myanmar–China borderland and in Mizoram, India, long confused with T. phayrei. Named after the mammalogist Guy Shortridge; it is listed as Endangered and its exact distribution is still being clarified.

children.name: Trachypithecus barbei
children.description: A langur of the Tenasserim Hills of Myanmar and Thailand, closely related to T. obscurus but darker and with less white facial marking. Named after the British naturalist C. H. Barbe; its status as a full species is recognised in recent classifications.

children.name: Trachypithecus crepusculus
children.description: Found in Thailand, Myanmar, and Yunnan Province, China, with a grey body and distinctly paler face than related taxa. Relatively common in suitable forest habitat but threatened by logging and the illegal wildlife trade.

children.name: Presbytis
children.description: Surilis or Sunda leaf monkeys are a diverse group of colobines endemic to the Sunda region — Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, Borneo, and adjacent islands. Most species are characterised by brightly coloured neonates, and several are distinguished only by subtle differences in pelage or crest shape.

children.name: Presbytis comata
children.description: An Endangered surili endemic to the western montane forests of Java, with a grizzled grey-brown pelage and upright crest. One of the most threatened primates of Java, restricted to a few fragmented forest blocks in the volcanic highlands.

children.name: Presbytis femoralis
children.description: Found in Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, and adjacent Riau Islands, with contrasting pale bands on the thighs. One of the more common surilis in Peninsular Malaysia but declining due to forest fragmentation; the Singapore population is Critically Endangered.

children.name: Presbytis frontata
children.description: A poorly known surili of the interior montane forests of Borneo, distinguished by a white forehead patch and dark face. It inhabits hill and montane dipterocarp forests above 300 m and is one of the least-studied Bornean primates.

children.name: Presbytis hosei
children.description: A large surili of montane and hill forests in northern Borneo, with four subspecies showing considerable variation in head colour. Named after the Sarawak naturalist Charles Hose; it is Vulnerable due to ongoing forest conversion.

children.name: Presbytis melalophos
children.description: The black-crested Sumatran langur is a species of primate in the family Cercopithecidae. It is endemic to Sumatra in Indonesia. Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical dry forests.

children.name: Presbytis rubicunda
children.description: One of the most strikingly coloured primates of Borneo, with a vivid chestnut-red coat and pale blue face. Common in lowland dipterocarp forest; it feeds heavily on seeds and young leaves and is one of the most frequently encountered primates in Bornean forests.

children.name: Presbytis siamensis
children.description: Distributed across Peninsular Malaysia, Riau, and Sumatra, with white outer thighs contrasting with the dark body. Common in lowland forest and sometimes found in disturbed habitats; multiple subspecies are recognised across its range.

children.name: Presbytis thomasi
children.description: Endemic to the forests of northern Sumatra, particularly north of Lake Toba, with a distinctive black-and-white banded coat. Named after Oldfield Thomas of the British Museum; it is Vulnerable due to logging and palm oil expansion.

children.name: Presbytis chrysomelas
children.description: Endemic to Sarawak and adjacent Brunei in northwestern Borneo, one of the most colourful surilis with orange and black patterned pelage. Endangered due to rapid deforestation in Sarawak for logging and plantation agriculture.

children.name: Presbytis natunae
children.description: An island endemic confined to Bunguran (Great Natuna) Island in the South China Sea, Indonesia. Vulnerable due to its extremely restricted range; forest on Bunguran has been substantially cleared for agriculture and human settlement.

children.name: Presbytis canicrus
children.description: A little-known surili found in East Kalimantan, Borneo, in the Kutai region, possibly endemic to the lowland forests of that province. Threatened by the massive deforestation of East Kalimantan for coal mining and oil palm.

children.name: Pygathrix
children.description: The douc langurs are among the most brilliantly coloured primates in the world, with multicoloured facial skin and vivid patterned coats. Confined to the forests of Vietnam, Laos, and Cambodia, all three species are severely threatened by the illegal wildlife trade and habitat loss.

children.name: Pygathrix nemaeus
children.description: One of the most vividly coloured primates on Earth, with a scarlet face, white whiskers, chestnut legs, and a white rump. Found in primary and secondary forests of Laos, Vietnam, and possibly Hainan; Endangered with populations decimated by Agent Orange and hunting.

children.name: Pygathrix nigripes
children.description: Found in the deciduous and semi-evergreen forests of southern Vietnam and Cambodia, distinguished from P. nemaeus by entirely black legs. Critically Endangered; the population in the Cardamom Mountains of Cambodia and nearby Vietnam forests is under severe hunting pressure.

children.name: Pygathrix cinerea
children.description: The rarest douc, restricted to the Central Highlands of Vietnam in Kon Tum, Gia Lai, and Quang Ngai Provinces. Critically Endangered with a total population estimated at 550–700 individuals; described formally only in 1997.

children.name: Rhinopithecus
children.description: Snub-nosed monkeys are specialised high-altitude colobines of China and adjacent Southeast Asia, bearing upturned nostrils without an external nasal bone. They are remarkable for inhabiting some of the coldest environments occupied by any primate, foraging in snow-covered forests.

children.name: Rhinopithecus roxellana
children.description: The most well-known snub-nosed monkey, with a spectacular golden-orange and black coat, found in the temperate forests of central and southwestern China. Lives in large bands of up to 600 individuals divided into one-male units; Endangered with approximately 15,000–20,000 individuals remaining.

children.name: Rhinopithecus bieti
children.description: The highest-altitude primate in the world, living in the coniferous forests of the Yunnan–Tibetan border at 3,000–4,500 m. Endangered with around 3,000 individuals; named after the French bishop Félix Biet who collected natural history specimens in Yunnan.

children.name: Rhinopithecus brelichi
children.description: Critically Endangered and confined to Fanjingshan (Fanjing Mountain) in Guizhou Province, China, with approximately 750 individuals in a single fragmented population. Named after the German trader A. von Brelich; one of the world's rarest primates and a flagship species for Fanjingshan National Nature Reserve.

children.name: Rhinopithecus avunculus
children.description: Critically Endangered and restricted to a few forest fragments in northern Vietnam, with a population of perhaps 250 individuals — one of the world's rarest primates. Its upturned nose, blue facial patches, and white ear tufts make it unmistakable.

children.name: Rhinopithecus strykeri
children.description: Described formally in 2010 from northern Myanmar's Kachin State, the most recently described snub-nosed monkey. Critically Endangered with an estimated population of 330–500 individuals; said by local hunters to sneeze audibly when it rains. Named after Jon Stryker.

children.name: Simias
children.description: A monotypic genus for the pig-tailed langur or simakobu, an unusual colobine endemic to the Mentawai Islands of Indonesia. Unlike most colobines, it has a short, pig-like tail and inhabits both primary and secondary forest.

children.name: Simias concolor
children.description: An Endangered colobine endemic to the Mentawai Islands (Siberut, Sipora, North Pagai, South Pagai) of Sumatra. Distinguished by a very short, nearly naked tail; heavily hunted by indigenous Mentawaians and threatened by logging across the archipelago.

children.name: Nasalis
children.description: The proboscis monkey is one of the most distinctive primates, the male bearing an enormous pendulous nose that amplifies vocalizations to attract mates. A highly specialised semi-aquatic species endemic to the mangroves and riverine forests of Borneo.

children.name: Nasalis larvatus
children.description: Endemic to Borneo and Endangered, the proboscis monkey is instantly recognisable by the male's enormous pendulous nose, used as a resonating chamber for loud honks. An expert swimmer that can cross wide rivers; its complex stomach ferments mangrove and nipa leaves that would be toxic to other primates.
